content:
# Luke Chapter 6 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **Sources:** EMET (Literal), LOGOS (Formal), PNEUMA (Dynamic) | **Base Text:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 91-93

---

**1** And it came to pass on a sabbath that he was passing through grain fields, and his disciples were plucking heads of grain and eating, rubbing them in their hands.

**2** And certain of the Pharisees said, "Why do you do what is not lawful on the sabbath?"

**3** And Jesus answered them, "Have you not read even this — what David did when he was hungry, he and those who were with him?

**4** How he entered the house of God and took the bread of the Presence and ate, and gave to those with him — which only the priests are permitted to eat?"

**5** And he said to them, "The Son of Man is lord of the sabbath."

**6** And it came to pass on another sabbath that he entered the synagogue and was teaching. And a man was there whose right hand was withered.

**7** And the scribes and the Pharisees were watching him closely, to see whether he would heal on the sabbath, so that they might find an accusation against him.

**8** But he knew their thoughts, and he said to the man with the withered hand, "Rise and stand in the midst." And he rose and stood.

**9** Then Jesus said to them, "I ask you: is it lawful on the sabbath to do good or to do evil — to save a life or to destroy it?"

**10** And having looked around at them all, he said to him, "Stretch out your hand." He did so, and his hand was restored.

**11** But they were filled with madness and began discussing with one another what they might do to Jesus.

**12** And it came to pass in those days that he went out to the mountain to pray, and he spent the whole night in prayer to God.

**13** And when day came, he called his disciples and chose from them twelve, whom he also named apostles:

**14** Simon, whom he also named Peter, and Andrew his brother; James and John; Philip and Bartholomew;

**15** Matthew and Thomas; James the son of Alphaeus, and Simon who was called the Zealot;

**16** Judas the son of James, and Judas Iscariot, who became a traitor.

**17** And having come down with them, he stood on a level place, with a large crowd of his disciples and a great multitude of people from all Judea and Jerusalem and the coastal region of Tyre and Sidon,

**18** who came to hear him and to be healed of their diseases. And those troubled by unclean spirits were being healed.

**19** And the whole crowd was seeking to touch him, because power was going out from him and was healing all.

**20** And he, lifting up his eyes upon his disciples, said:

"Blessed are you who are poor, for yours is the kingdom of God.

**21** Blessed are you who hunger now, for you shall be satisfied. Blessed are you who weep now, for you shall laugh.

**22** Blessed are you when people hate you, and when they exclude you and reproach you and cast out your name as evil, on account of the Son of Man.

**23** Rejoice in that day and leap for joy, for behold, your reward is great in heaven; for their fathers did the same to the prophets.

**24** But woe to you who are rich, for you have received your consolation.

**25** Woe to you who are full now, for you shall hunger. Woe to you who laugh now, for you shall mourn and weep.

**26** Woe to you when all people speak well of you, for their fathers did the same to the false prophets.

**27** But I say to you who hear: Love your enemies, do good to those who hate you,

**28** bless those who curse you, pray for those who mistreat you.

**29** To the one who strikes you on the cheek, offer the other also; and from the one who takes your cloak, do not withhold your tunic either.

**30** Give to everyone who asks of you, and from the one who takes what is yours, do not demand it back.

**31** And as you wish that others should do to you, do to them likewise.

**32** If you love those who love you, what credit is that to you? For even sinners love those who love them.

**33** And if you do good to those who do good to you, what credit is that to you? Even sinners do the same.

**34** And if you lend to those from whom you expect to receive, what credit is that to you? Even sinners lend to sinners, expecting to be repaid in full.

**35** But love your enemies, and do good, and lend, expecting nothing in return; and your reward shall be great, and you shall be sons of the Most High, for he is kind to the ungrateful and the evil.

**36** Be merciful, just as your Father is merciful.

**37** Do not judge, and you shall not be judged. Do not condemn, and you shall not be condemned. Forgive, and you shall be forgiven.

**38** Give, and it shall be given to you — good measure, pressed down, shaken together, and overflowing — it shall be poured into your lap. For with the measure you use, it shall be measured back to you.

**39** And he told them a parable: "Can a blind man lead a blind man? Will they not both fall into a pit?

**40** A disciple is not above his teacher, but everyone who is fully trained will be like his teacher.

**41** Why do you look at the speck in your brother's eye, but do not notice the beam in your own eye?

**42** How can you say to your brother, 'Brother, let me take out the speck that is in your eye,' when you yourself do not see the beam in your own eye? Hypocrite! First take out the beam from your own eye, and then you will see clearly to take out the speck from your brother's eye.

**43** For no good tree produces rotten fruit, nor does a rotten tree produce good fruit.

**44** For each tree is known by its own fruit. Figs are not gathered from thorns, nor are grapes harvested from a bramble bush.

**45** The good person out of the good treasure of the heart brings forth good, and the evil person out of evil brings forth evil; for out of the abundance of the heart the mouth speaks.

**46** Why do you call me 'Lord, Lord,' and do not do what I say?

**47** Everyone who comes to me and hears my words and does them — I will show you what that person is like:

**48** They are like a man building a house, who dug deep and laid a foundation upon the rock. And when a flood came, the river burst against that house and could not shake it, because it had been well built.

**49** But the one who heard and did not act is like a man who built a house upon the ground without a foundation, against which the river burst, and immediately it collapsed, and the ruin of that house was great.

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

### Consensus Methodology

SOPHIA evaluated all 49 verses across EMET (literal), LOGOS (formal), and PNEUMA (dynamic), with reference to the Codex Sinaiticus manuscript images (pages 91-93). The guiding principle: fidelity to the Greek source as preserved in Sinaiticus, rendered in clear, dignified English that preserves both theological precision and natural readability.

### Key Consensus Decisions

1. **6:1 — "heads of grain"**: SOPHIA follows LOGOS and PNEUMA ("heads of grain") over EMET's more archaic "ears-of-grain." The Greek stachyas means the spike or head of a cereal plant; "heads of grain" is accurate and avoids archaism.

2. **6:4 — "bread of the Presence"**: SOPHIA adopts LOGOS's "bread of the Presence" over EMET's "loaves of the setting-forth" and PNEUMA's "sacred bread of offering." The Greek artous tes protheseos translates the Hebrew lehem happanim; "bread of the Presence" is the standard scholarly rendering that preserves theological meaning.

3. **6:10 — "restored" vs. "completely restored"**: SOPHIA follows EMET and LOGOS ("was restored") over PNEUMA's "completely restored." Sinaiticus reads apekatestatheh without the expansion "healthy as the other" found in some manuscripts. The simpler rendering honors the manuscript.

4. **6:11 — "madness"**: SOPHIA retains "madness" (EMET, LOGOS) rather than PNEUMA's "fury." The Greek anoias means "senselessness/mindlessness," which "madness" captures more precisely than "fury."

5. **6:12 — "prayer to God"**: SOPHIA follows LOGOS and PNEUMA ("prayer to God") over EMET's more literal "the prayer of God." While the Greek en te proseucheh tou theou is ambiguous (objective genitive or reference to a prayer-house), "prayer to God" communicates the most likely sense clearly.

6. **6:16 — "traitor" vs. "betrayer"**: SOPHIA follows LOGOS and PNEUMA ("traitor") over EMET's "betrayer." The Greek prodotes means one who hands over or betrays; "traitor" carries the fuller weight of the term in English.

7. **6:20 — "Blessed are you who are poor"**: SOPHIA follows PNEUMA's second-person formulation ("you who are poor") which accurately reflects Sinaiticus. Luke's beatitudes are addressed directly in the second person, unlike Matthew's third-person form. All three agents agree Sinaiticus omits Matthew's "in spirit."

8. **6:24-26 — "Woe" vs. "How terrible"**: SOPHIA retains the traditional "Woe" (EMET, LOGOS) over PNEUMA's "How terrible." While PNEUMA's rendering captures the grief dimension of ouai, "Woe" preserves the prophetic register that links Jesus's words to the Old Testament woe oracles and is widely recognized.

9. **6:29 — "cloak...tunic"**: All three agents agree on Luke's distinctive order (outer garment first, then inner), reversing Matthew's sequence. SOPHIA follows LOGOS's "cloak...tunic" as the clearest rendering of himation...chitona.

10. **6:32-34 — "credit" (charis)**: SOPHIA follows LOGOS and PNEUMA ("credit") over EMET's "grace." While charis often means "grace," in this rhetorical context it functions as "credit" or "what is praiseworthy" — the point is that loving only those who reciprocate earns no distinction.

11. **6:35 — "sons of the Most High"**: SOPHIA retains "sons" (EMET, LOGOS) over PNEUMA's "children." The Greek huioi is specifically masculine plural; while "children" is more inclusive in modern English, "sons" preserves the Semitic idiom (ben/huios) that denotes likeness to and derivation from a quality. A footnote-worthy decision, but "sons" maintains textual fidelity.

12. **6:36 — "merciful" vs. "compassionate"**: SOPHIA adopts "merciful" (EMET, LOGOS) over PNEUMA's "compassionate." The Greek oiktirmones encompasses both mercy and compassion; "merciful" aligns with the longstanding translation tradition and pairs naturally with "Be merciful, just as your Father is merciful."

13. **6:37 — "Forgive" vs. "Release"**: SOPHIA follows LOGOS and PNEUMA ("Forgive") over EMET's "Release." The Greek apoluete literally means "release/set free," but in this ethical context "forgive" communicates the intended sense to English readers while maintaining the parallelism with "judge" and "condemn."

14. **6:38 — "lap" vs. "bosom"**: SOPHIA follows LOGOS and PNEUMA ("lap") over EMET's "bosom." The Greek kolpos refers to the fold of the garment used as a carrying pouch; "lap" is the more natural modern equivalent.

15. **6:41-42 — "speck...beam"**: SOPHIA follows EMET and LOGOS ("speck...beam") over PNEUMA's "splinter...plank." The Greek karphos...dokos describes a tiny particle versus a massive construction timber; "speck and beam" preserves the traditional pairing while maintaining the hyperbolic contrast.

16. **6:48 — "dug deep"**: SOPHIA follows PNEUMA's compressed "dug deep" over EMET's "dug and deepened" and LOGOS's "dug and deepened." While Sinaiticus does read two verbs (eskapsen kai ebathunen), "dug deep" captures both actions in natural English without losing meaning. The double-verb structure is noted here for transparency.

17. **6:48 — "because it had been well built"**: All three agents agree on following Sinaiticus's reading (dia to kalos oikodomesthai auten) over the variant "for it had been founded upon the rock." This is a significant textual decision where Sinaiticus emphasizes quality of construction rather than restating the foundation.

18. **6:49 — "upon the ground"**: All three agents correctly follow Sinaiticus's "upon the ground" (epi ten gen) rather than Matthew's "upon sand." Luke's contrast is rock versus bare earth, not rock versus sand.

### Manuscript Verification

The three Sinaiticus images (pages 91-93) were examined. The text is written in continuous Greek uncial script across four columns per page. The chapter begins mid-page on page 91 (from approximately Lk 5:29 onward through Lk 6:12), continues across page 92 (Lk 6:19-6:40), and concludes on page 93 (Lk 6:45 through the beginning of Luke 7). Key readings confirmed from the manuscript:
- EN SABBATW (6:1) without DEUTEROPRWTW
- APEKATESTAQH (6:10) without the "healthy as the other" expansion
- MAKARIOI OI PTWXOI (6:20) without TW PNEUMATI
- MHDEN APELPIZONTEC (6:35)
- DIA TO KALWC OIKODOMHCQAI AUTHN (6:48)

---

## Disagreement Log

| Verse | Issue | EMET (Literal) | LOGOS (Formal) | PNEUMA (Dynamic) | SOPHIA Decision | Rationale |
|-------|-------|----------------|----------------|-------------------|-----------------|-----------|
| 6:1 | "ears-of-grain" vs "heads of grain" | ears-of-grain | heads of grain | heads of grain | heads of grain | Modern standard; avoids archaism; 2-to-1 |
| 6:4 | Bread terminology | loaves of the setting-forth | bread of the Presence | sacred bread of offering | bread of the Presence | Standard scholarly rendering of artous tes protheseos |
| 6:10 | Restoration scope | was restored | was restored | completely restored | was restored | Sinaiticus lacks expansion; 2-to-1 |
| 6:11 | anoia rendering | madness | madness | fury | madness | Greek = "mindlessness"; "madness" more precise; 2-to-1 |
| 6:12 | Prayer phrase | the prayer of God | prayer to God | prayer to God | prayer to God | Objective genitive reading clearer; 2-to-1 |
| 6:16 | prodotes rendering | betrayer | traitor | traitor | traitor | "Traitor" carries full weight; 2-to-1 |
| 6:20 | Beatitude person | the poor (3rd) | the poor (3rd) | you who are poor (2nd) | you who are poor (2nd) | Sinaiticus uses 2nd person; PNEUMA most accurate to Greek |
| 6:24-26 | ouai rendering | Woe | Woe | How terrible | Woe | Prophetic register; OT echo; 2-to-1 |
| 6:32-34 | charis rendering | grace | credit | credit | credit | Contextual meaning = merit/credit, not theological grace; 2-to-1 |
| 6:35 | huioi rendering | sons | sons | children | sons | Greek huioi; Semitic idiom preserved; 2-to-1 |
| 6:36 | oiktirmones rendering | merciful | merciful | compassionate | merciful | Traditional; encompasses both senses; 2-to-1 |
| 6:37 | apoluete rendering | Release | Forgive | Forgive | Forgive | Ethical context; English clarity; 2-to-1 |
| 6:38 | kolpos rendering | bosom | lap | lap | lap | Modern equivalent; 2-to-1 |
| 6:41-42 | karphos/dokos rendering | speck/beam | speck/beam | splinter/plank | speck/beam | Traditional pairing; maintains contrast; 2-to-1 |
| 6:45 | Gender language | The good man | The good man | A good person | The good person | Balance: retains article, uses inclusive "person" |
| 6:48 | Double verb | dug and deepened | dug and deepened | dug deep | dug deep | Natural English; no meaning loss; noted in council |
| 6:49 | rhegma rendering | ruin...was great | ruin...was great | destruction...was total | ruin...was great | Greek rhegma = "ruin/wreck"; "great" for mega; 2-to-1 |
